stories.title: Blake Investment wins bid for $8B Tropicana Field redevelopment
stories.excerpt: A team led by Blake Investment Partners won a bid to redevelop St. Petersburg’s Historic Gas Plant District, home to Tropicana Field and one of the most sought-after development opportunities in the Southeastern United States. St. Petersburg Mayor Ken Welch selected Blake Investment Partners, led by St. Petersburg native Thompson Blake, to redevelop the 86-acre site, the Tampa Bay Times reported. Blake’s team includes the Related Group and hedge fund Elliott Investment Management. The firm beat out the competing finalist with “The Burg Bid,” a proposal to purchase 58 acres for $275 million and pour $8.1 billion into the project. […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Blake Investment Partners, Related Group, Elliott Management win $8.1B Tropicana Field redevelopment in St. Petersburg, 86 acres
Project includes 3,600 affordable units (for-sale and rental) plus retail, cultural, museum anchors; two-decade buildout expected
Demolition of existing stadium despite $58M hurricane repairs; third developer selected for site in six years
stories.summary: Blake Investment Partners' consortium won a competitive bid to redevelop the 86-acre Historic Gas Plant District in St. Petersburg, Florida, home to Tropicana Field. The $8.1 billion project plans 3,600 income-restricted housing units (both ownership and rental), parks, retail, and cultural anchors over a 20-year development horizon. The proposal prioritizes affordability above competing bids and will require demolition of the existing stadium.
stories.impact: This signals strong institutional capital appetite for large-scale, long-term mixed-use redevelopment in secondary Florida markets and a policy shift toward affordable-housing-forward projects. For Tampa Bay metro multifamily: 3,600 restricted units may suppress near-term market-rate demand if they achieve deep affordability, but the two-decade timeline and scale mean limited near-term supply pressure; however, the sheer scale and mixed-use anchor tenants (museums, cultural uses) will drive population and employment inflow over time, supporting longer-term rental demand in surrounding submarkets.

stories.title: Stevenson affiliate cuts unit count at proposed Glendale multifamily project
stories.excerpt: A developer is downsizing its proposed multifamily development in Glendale more than two years after the plans first surfaced.  A new application filed with the City of Glendale outlines new plans from 550 Riverdale LLC, the owner of the development site at 2748 San Fernando Road in the city’s Pacific-Edison neighborhood, Urbanize Los Angeles reported. The LLC is linked in state business records to David Stevenson, president of Glendale-based Stevenson Real Estate Services, and the real estate firm’s Glendale address. In 2024, the Robert and Stefanie Stevenson Family Trust — the property owner at the time — filed an application […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Stevenson affiliate downsizes Glendale project from 249 units to 229 units; still pursuing 7-story entitlement vs. 6-story zoning
Developer offering 27 affordable units via density bonus; project seeks state RHNA relief in tight supply market
Glendale has three years left to hit 13,425-unit state housing mandate; active development pipeline signals compliance headroom
stories.summary: 550 Riverdale LLC, linked to Glendale-based Stevenson Real Estate Services, reduced its proposed multifamily project at 2748 San Fernando Road from 249 to 229 units and is pursuing a density bonus to build seven stories instead of the zoned six. The developer is offering 27 affordable units in exchange for entitlements. Glendale must deliver 13,425 new units by 2029 to meet state RHNA goals and currently has entitlements progressing.
stories.impact: The unit count reduction despite market tailwinds (tight RHNA timeline, investor demand) suggests land/entitlement cost inflation or lender/equity conservatism on smaller projects. For greater Los Angeles: Glendale's active development pipeline indicates the city will likely meet RHNA, reducing regional affordability-driven supply pressure. However, continued downsizing by individual projects hints at tighter project-level economics, foreshadowing slower incremental supply delivery if construction costs or financing conditions deteriorate further.

stories.title: LCOR lands $193M construction loan for Edgewater tower amid flurry of apartment projects
stories.excerpt: LCOR scored a $192.5 million construction loan for a 544-unit luxury apartment tower in Edgewater, as developers are excitedly pursuing multifamily projects across South Florida, despite a lingering oversupply.  New York-based LCOR started construction of a 39-story building at 1775 Biscayne Boulevard in Miami, according to the developer’s news release. The 1.1-acre site is a block from waterfront and Margaret Pace Park. The construction loan amounts to $354,000 per unit. Designed by ODP Architect, with interiors by Asprea Studio, the tower will include more than 40,000 square feet of amenities, over 10,000 square feet of ground-floor retail and a 448-space […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: LCOR secures $192.5M construction loan (39-story, 544-unit luxury tower) in Miami's Edgewater; $354K per unit loan size
Natixis financing; maturity 2029; substantial completion Q4 2028; $49M land acquisition in 2022 on 1.1-acre site
South Florida 2024 delivered 18,600 units with ~20% oversupply vs. leasing; investors still underwriting despite rent declines
stories.summary: LCOR closed a $192.5 million construction loan for a 544-unit luxury tower at 1775 Biscayne Boulevard in Edgewater, Miami, led by Natixis with a consortium of lenders and maturing in 2029. The loan amounts to $354,000 per unit. The developer acquired the site for $49 million in 2022 and expects completion in Q4 2028. This comes amid South Florida's oversupply crisis, with 18,600 units delivered in 2024 outpacing net leasing by roughly 20 percent.
stories.impact: LCOR's ability to secure nearly $193M in construction financing despite documented oversupply signals lenders' selective willingness to fund trophy/waterfront assets with strong sponsorship and amenities, even in distressed markets. For Miami Edgewater submarket: luxury supply will worsen near-term rental declines, but the three-year construction timeline means delayed delivery; more importantly, this demonstrates capital still flows to prime assets, which may crowd out secondary product and keep base-market rents under pressure longer. Developers should expect financing scrutiny and equity calls on non-waterfront, non-luxury product in South Florida for the next 12–18 months.

stories.title: David Martin in talks to join Related, BH on Hollywood Beach Resort redevelopment
stories.excerpt: Developer David Martin is in talks to join the Related Group and BH Group on their planned redevelopment of the Hollywood Beach Resort, sources told The Real Deal.  The Pérez family’s Coconut Grove-based Related and Isaac and Liat Toledano’s Aventura-based BH Group are under contract to acquire the 4.5-acre property at 101 North Ocean Drive, as previously reported by TRD. The Chetrit Group is selling the century-old resort. A source said the property is under contract for $100 million.  Martin, CEO of Terra Group declined to comment through a spokesperson. In a statement, BH named Terra and Related as its […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Terra Group (David Martin, CEO) in talks to join Related/BH on Hollywood Beach Resort redevelopment; $100M purchase price for 4.5 acres
398-unit existing resort; century-old property; Chetrit Group seller; city commissioner voted against vested development rights confirmation
Terra and BH also partnering on luxury condo (West Palm Beach 1.6 acres, $100M acquisition)
stories.summary: David Martin's Terra Group is in negotiations to join the Pérez family's Related Group and BH Group on redeveloping the Hollywood Beach Resort, a 4.5-acre oceanfront property under contract for $100 million from the Chetrit Group. The development involves redeveloping an existing 398-unit resort. A city commissioner voted against confirming the property's vested development rights, citing deferred maintenance. Terra and BH are also co-developing a luxury condo project in West Palm Beach.
stories.impact: Entitlement friction (vested rights rejection) and century-old asset physical condition will lengthen predevelopment and increase soft costs; this is a test case for oceanfront resort-to-multifamily conversions in Hollywood Beach. For South Florida coastal markets: the deal size and sponsor quality (Related/BH scale) suggest strong belief in beachfront rental/condo demand in lower-density coastal corridors, but regulatory/neighborhood resistance may be rising. Developers should expect tighter scrutiny on coastal and heritage properties; conversion upside may be offset by extended entitlement and remediation timelines.

stories.title: Whirlybird dreams: Ken Griffin wants helipad for Miami Beach megayacht marina
stories.excerpt: Billionaire Citadel founder Ken Griffin has already conquered the land and the sea in Miami Beach. His next target? The air.  City commissioners approved Griffin’s massive private megayacht marina at 120 MacArthur Causeway a few months ago, but now Griffin wants to chopper in. He submitted a request for a private, waterfront helipad at his sprawling Terminal Island development, the Miami Herald reported. The proposal, sponsored by Miami Beach Commissioner Joseph Magazine, is navigating the city’s Land Use and Sustainability Committee and the Planning Board. To clear the runway for Griffin, officials would need to amend Miami Beach’s land development […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Ken Griffin seeks private helipad amendment for Terminal Island 3.5-acre megayacht marina (120 MacArthur Causeway), Miami Beach
Megayacht marina approved 'in recent months'; helipad would be accessory use in industrial zone; Griffin also owns $169M in Star Island parcels nearby
Non-residential (maritime/industrial) use case; minimal direct bearing on multifamily demand
stories.summary: Ken Griffin, billionaire Citadel founder, is pursuing an amendment to Miami Beach's land development regulations to permit a private helipad at his Terminal Island megayacht marina development. The marina itself was recently approved; the helipad would serve as an accessory use within the industrial zoning district. Griffin acquired the 3.5-acre site from Related Group and separately owns $169 million in nearby Star Island residential parcels.
stories.impact: Minimal direct multifamily relevance. Helipad approval would enhance ultra-luxury lifestyle amenities in Miami Beach's most exclusive waterfront enclave, but the land use is maritime/industrial, not residential. Indirectly, it signals continued ultra-high-net-worth capital flowing to Miami Beach and deepening the luxury resort lifestyle ecosystem, which may support premium multifamily leasing in nearby South Pointe or Brickell submarkets, though the effect is indirect and long-term.

stories.title: Houston’s office glut is fueling a demolition trend for mostly vacant properties
stories.excerpt: As the Houston office market struggles to recover at the same rate as other similar cities, sometimes just tearing the buildings down is better for everyone involved.  Record low office vacancies and a need for private demolition has led to more demand than capital available for demolition work, according to Bisnow. Houston had an office vacancy rate of 26.8 percent, according to the outlet, a staggering figure attributable to old or obsolete office buildings and the pandemic ripple effect on employee hybrid schedules.  Vacant or near-vacant buildings all over the city can’t be demolished due to debt and lenders that […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Houston office vacancy 26.8%; demolition demand exceeds contractor capacity; obsolete stock cannot be financed/demolished due to lender holdups
Positive absorption in some submarkets (Galleria +200K SF in 2025), but overall annual absorption remains negative; distress/conversion deals dominant
Owner-occupancy emerging as alternative to traditional leasing; office-to-multifamily conversion slower than in other Sunbelt metros
stories.summary: Houston's office market suffers 26.8 percent vacancy, driving record demolition demand that outpaces contractor capacity. Lenders and debt holders prevent demolition of obsolete properties, leaving empty buildings standing. While some submarkets (Galleria area) showed positive absorption in 2025, annual absorption overall remains negative. Distress sales and conversions dominate transaction volume; owner-occupancy is increasingly popular as an alternative to traditional leasing.
stories.impact: High office vacancies and constrained demolition create long-term overhang on Houston commercial real estate values and tax revenue, but open a clear window for office-to-multifamily conversion developers with debt solutions and conversion expertise. For Houston multifamily: conversion opportunity is real but financing constraints on existing office holders (lender resistance to modification) slow supply of available shells. Developers should target smaller (50K–150K SF) older office buildings where repositioning to apartments is capital-efficient and where land value doesn't justify pure land-play demolition.

stories.title: Inflationary Concerns Send School Shoppers Back to Discount Stores: Report
stories.excerpt: Parents’ back-to-school budgets are up nearly 12 percent this year, according to JLL’s 2026 Back-to-School Survey, and that’s good news for discount stores. The report, released Thursday, saw the dollar store sector enter the survey’s top 10 list of parents’ most-cited shopping destinations. The sector’s rising star comes as Americans continue to tighten belts in the face of inflation. The brokerage’s online survey collected responses from 1,022 U.S. parents with children between the ages of 5 and 18. Its release coincides with the July back-to-school shopping surge, when 27 percent of surveyed parents plan to check off their children’s classroom wish lists. Over 90 percent of parents plan to shop at physical stores this year, according to the survey, and dollar stores and mass merchants dominated their spending plans. The popularity of cheaper retailers such as Dollar Tree and Walmart are no surprise –– the report found school shopping budgets rising 11.7 percent from last year, up to an average of $489 per child. The uptick runs well ahead of the current U.S. inflation rate of around 4 percent. “Families are seeking retailers that respect their time and budgets, which explains why 68.7 percent cite saving money, and 35.1 percent prioritize finding the full school list in one place,” James Cook, JLL’s Americas director of retail research, said in a statement. The largest budget hike was found among middle-income families, who raised their spending expectations roughly 20 percent this year to $495 per child. The number of discretionary items on parents’ shopping lists fell in kind for the second consecutive year, meaning fewer fidget spinners and novelty pens will be landing in shopping carts. Consumers are shopping more strategically, the report said, with stores like Dollar General and Dollar Tree gaining ground for their discounts, and mass retailers like Walmart taking the top spot for their one-stop-shop convenience. Walmart popularity r
bullets: Back-to-school budgets up 11.7% YoY to $489/child; dollar stores (Dollar Tree, Family Dollar) entered top-10 shopping destinations; 90%+ in-store shopping preference
Inflation concerns driving consumer shift toward discount retailers despite nominal budget increases; survey of 1,022 U.S. parents, ages 5–18
No direct multifamily relevance
stories.summary: JLL's 2026 Back-to-School Survey found that U.S. parents' spending budgets rose 11.7 percent year-over-year to an average of $489 per child, yet consumer behavior shifted decisively toward discount stores like Dollar Tree and Walmart due to inflation concerns. Dollar stores entered the survey's top 10 shopping destinations for the first time. Over 90 percent of parents plan to shop in physical retail in 2026.
stories.impact: No material bearing on multifamily development, financing, or site selection. Signals broader consumer pullback and inflationary headwinds that may dampen household formation and discretionary spending on housing, potentially suppressing rent growth in secondary markets, but effect is indirect and already priced into underwriting.

stories.title: CBRE Opens Hospitality-Inspired Office in Columbus
stories.excerpt: CBRE announced the opening of its new 19,500-square-foot office on the fifth floor at 10 W. Nationwide Blvd., a trophy office building within the Arena District owned and developed by Nationwide Realty Investors in downtown Columbus, Ohio. The firm has relocated from 200 Civic Center Drive. The new office is part of CBRE’s Workplace360 program, which showcases technology and a wide variety of collaborative spaces designed to support hybrid working. “As one of Columbus’s top trophy office buildings, 10 W. Nationwide Blvd. is an ideal home, and CBRE is proud to be its first tenant,” said CBRE’s Michael Copella, Executive Managing Director and Market Leader, Central Ohio. “The office design reflects the spirit of Columbus and its distinctive culture while highlighting the scope of CBRE’s capabilities.” New technology is installed throughout the office to support remote and in-person collaboration between employees. The office’s design reflects the unique character of Columbus, drawing inspiration from bespoke hotels and hospitality-focused experiences. The post CBRE Opens Hospitality-Inspired Office in Columbus appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: CBRE opened 19,500-SF Workplace360 office at trophy building (10 W. Nationwide Blvd., Arena District, Columbus); relocated from Civic Center
Design inspired by hospitality; hybrid-work tech integration; Nationwide Realty Investors ownership; first tenant in building
Columbus office market stabilization signal; no multifamily impact
stories.summary: CBRE relocated its Columbus office to 10 W. Nationwide Blvd., a trophy office building in downtown's Arena District owned by Nationwide Realty Investors. The 19,500-square-foot space is the building's first tenant and features Workplace360 design with hospitality-inspired aesthetics and hybrid-work technology. CBRE relocated from 200 Civic Center Drive.
stories.impact: Minimal multifamily relevance. Signals selective leasing appetite in trophy downtown Columbus office product and corporate willingness to invest in amenity-rich, tech-enabled spaces, but does not directly affect apartment demand or supply in Columbus.

stories.title: JLL Arranges Sale of Lincoln Park Retail Center to Curbline Properties
stories.excerpt: JLL has secured the sale of Clybourn Center, a 33,140-square-foot unanchored strip center strategically located in Chicago’s Lincoln Park neighborhood. JLL’s Capital Market Investment and Sales Advisory team, representing the seller, was led by Managing Director Michael Nieder and Director Brian Page. Leasing efforts at the center have been spearheaded by Steve Schwartz and his team. The buyer was Curbline Properties. Clybourn Center is strategically positioned at 2029-2073 N. Clybourn Ave., across from Foundry Park, a transformational $1 billion-plus mixed-use redevelopment project. When complete, Foundry Park will deliver approximately 3,700 residential units, 350,000 square feet of office space, 420,000 square feet of retail space, 250,000 square feet of hospitality space, and 12 acres of open space. The 91.2% occupied property features 86 surface-level parking spaces, which is a rare amenity for street-front retail in this dense urban corridor. Clybourn Center’s tenant mix is anchored by medical and wellness uses, with 38% of the gross leasable area occupied by medical tenants. The post JLL Arranges Sale of Lincoln Park Retail Center to Curbline Properties appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: JLL sells Clybourn Center (33,140 SF unanchored strip retail, Lincoln Park, Chicago) to Curbline Properties; 91.2% occupied, 38% medical tenants
Adjacent to Foundry Park ($1B+ mixed-use); 3,700 residential units, 350K SF office, 420K SF retail planned for Foundry Park
No direct multifamily impact; retail property sale in context of major mixed-use redevelopment
stories.summary: JLL facilitated the sale of Clybourn Center, a 33,140-square-foot unanchored retail strip in Chicago's Lincoln Park neighborhood, to Curbline Properties. The property is 91.2 percent occupied with medical/wellness anchors and sits directly across from Foundry Park, a transformational $1 billion-plus mixed-use development that will deliver 3,700 residential units, 350,000 square feet of office, and 420,000 square feet of retail.
stories.impact: Curbline's acquisition of complementary street-front retail opposite a major 3,700-unit mixed-use development suggests investor confidence in the Foundry Park project's ability to drive foot traffic and commercial rents in Lincoln Park. For Chicago multifamily: Foundry Park's delivery (timeline not specified) will drive significant renter supply and density in this submarket; existing retail operators like Curbline are positioning ahead of that influx, indicating strong commercial stakeholder belief in the project's success.

stories.title: MCA Realty Acquires 138K-SF Seattle-Area Industrial Portfolio
stories.excerpt: MCA Realty, Inc., a real estate investment company based in California, announced the acquisition of West Valley Distribution Center, a three-building 138,296-square-foot multi-tenant industrial portfolio located in Kent, Washington. The firm acquired the property from Tamarisk West Valley LLC with its MCA Realty Industrial Growth Fund II. MCA now owns three industrial properties encompassing 268,843 square feet in the Seattle MSA. Newmark’s Andrew Briner, Aaron Banks and Kevin Shannon represented both MCA Realty and the seller in the transaction. The firm’s Taylor Hoff and Thad Mallory have been retained to oversee leasing at the property.MCA Realty will make significant exterior and interior improvements. It also plans to reconfigure the suites. “This acquisition reflects our view that the Kent Valley will remain an attractive location for industrial occupants,” said Tyler Mattox, Principal at MCA Realty. “The property’s infill location, durable tenant demand, and upside potential make it a compelling addition to our portfolio.” Hear from the West Coast’s Leading Industrial Real Estate Experts | August 20Gain firsthand insight from the executives, investors, and developers navigating today’s industrial market. From shifting capital flows and debt trends to evolving occupier demand and development strategies, take part in the conversations shaping the future of industrial real estate. Register now: www.connectindustrialwest2026.com The post MCA Realty Acquires 138K-SF Seattle-Area Industrial Portfolio appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: MCA Realty acquires West Valley Distribution Center (138K SF, three buildings, Kent, Washington); adds to 268K SF Seattle MSA industrial portfolio
MCA Realty Industrial Growth Fund II; planned exterior/interior improvements and suite reconfiguration; infill location, durable tenant demand cited
Industrial acquisition; no multifamily relevance
stories.summary: MCA Realty, a California-based investment firm, acquired West Valley Distribution Center, a 138,296-square-foot, three-building industrial portfolio in Kent, Washington, through its Industrial Growth Fund II. The acquisition brings MCA's Seattle-area industrial holdings to 268,843 square feet. MCA plans capital improvements and suite reconfiguration; Newmark brokered the transaction.
stories.impact: Industrial and multifamily markets are adjacent but largely separate capital flows. No direct multifamily supply/demand impact. However, Kent Valley's appeal to industrial capital (infill, durable demand) mirrors supply-chain demand that supports wage growth for blue-collar workers, which can underpin middle-market multifamily demand in Seattle area if reflected in job growth.

stories.title: Rithm Capital Lands $515M Financing for Midtown Office Tower
stories.excerpt: Cushman & Wakefield and Newmark announced that their respective Equity, Debt & Structured Finance teams advised Rithm Capital in securing $515 million in fixed-rate financing for 31 W. 52nd St., a 29-story, 785,000-square-foot office tower in Midtown Manhattan. The Cushman & Wakefield team of Gideon Gil, Zach Kraft and Cecelia Galligan co-brokered the financing alongside Newmark’s Jordan Roeschlaub, Nick Scribani, Adam Spies, Adam Doneger, Tim Polglase, Dan Axelson and Jack Fenton. Led by Wells Fargo, the financing package consists of a $415-million senior mortgage, a $40 million B-Note and a $60-million mezzanine loan. The lending group also includes Bank of America, Barclays, Citi, Goldman Sachs and JPMorgan. The refinancing follows Rithm Capital’s acquisition of the larger Paramount Group office portfolio. “Securing financing of this scale from a consortium of leading lenders demonstrates continued confidence in well-positioned office assets in New York City’s premier business districts,” said Gil. “As capital markets continue to evolve, institutional lenders remain focused on high-quality properties backed by experienced ownership and strong long-term fundamentals.” The post Rithm Capital Lands $515M Financing for Midtown Office Tower appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Rithm Capital secures $515M fixed-rate financing (31 W. 52nd St., Midtown Manhattan; 785K SF, 29-story office tower)
Wells Fargo lead; consortium includes BofA, Barclays, Citi, Goldman Sachs, JPMorgan; senior $415M + $40M B-note + $60M mezz; refinance follows Paramount Group acquisition
Trophy office asset; demonstrates lender appetite for prime Manhattan despite sector headwinds
stories.summary: Rithm Capital secured $515 million in fixed-rate financing for 31 W. 52nd Street, a 785,000-square-foot, 29-story office tower in Midtown Manhattan, led by Wells Fargo with a consortium of major banks. The financing package comprises a $415 million senior mortgage, $40 million B-note, and $60 million mezzanine loan. This refinancing follows Rithm's acquisition of the larger Paramount Group office portfolio.
stories.impact: Demonstrates institutional lender selectivity: trophy, occupied office assets in prime CBDs still access capital at favorable terms, but secondary/lower-occupancy office assets remain starved of refinancing and are conversion/demolition candidates. For multifamily developers in major metros: office-to-residential conversion pipelines remain viable but should focus on Class B/C buildings where financing is unavailable; trophy properties are being held and refinanced by institutional owners, not converted.

stories.title: Auto Parts Distributor Signs $42M Lease in Southern California
stories.excerpt: An auto parts distribution company is expanding in Southern California and has signed a lease to take over a 522,267-square-foot industrial building in the Inland Empire. The lease agreement with landlord Invesco is valued at more than $42 million, according to Daum Commercial Real Estate Services, which announced the deal Thursday and represented the tenant alongside Lara CRE. Daum said the lease represents a significant expansion of the tenant’s Southern California distribution footprint, but did not disclose the company’s name or the length of the lease. The property at 16380 Euclid Avenue is Building 3 at the Chino South Industrial Center in Chino, Calif. It was constructed in 2014 with 32-foot clear heights and dock-high loading bays. Chino’s direct industrial vacancy was at 3.24 percent in the first quarter with limited availability for large distribution space, according to Daum’s research. Daum’s Gus Andros represented the tenant along with Nathan Lara, Andrew Lara, Rudy Lara and Jack Nersesian of Lara CRE. Steve Bellitti, Tom Taylor, Joey Jones and Evelyn Manning of Colliers represented Invesco. Colliers did not immediately return a request for comment. Gregory Cornfield can be reached at gcornfield@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Auto parts distributor leases 522,267 SF industrial building (Building 3, Chino South Industrial Center, Inland Empire) for $42M+; landlord Invesco
16,380 Euclid Ave., Chino; built 2014, 32-ft clear heights, dock-high bays; Chino direct vacancy 3.24% Q1 2026; tenant not disclosed
Lease signals strong industrial/logistics demand; no multifamily impact
stories.summary: An undisclosed auto parts distribution company signed a $42 million-plus lease for a 522,267-square-foot industrial building (Building 3 at Chino South Industrial Center) in Chino, California. The property, built in 2014 with 32-foot clear heights and dock-high loading, is landlord-owned by Invesco. Chino's direct industrial vacancy stands at 3.24 percent as of Q1 2026, indicating tight supply for large distribution users.
stories.impact: Industrial leasing strength (3.24% vacancy in Inland Empire) underpins continued wage/employment growth for logistics workers, supporting multifamily demand for workforce housing in Chino, Ontario, and Moreno Valley submarkets. However, this is an industrial story, not a multifamily supply or capital signal. Developers should note: strong industrial leasing and low vacancy in Inland Empire suggest sustained labor demand and renter population, supporting market-rate and workforce multifamily in these submarkets over next 2–3 years.

stories.title: Northmarq Secures Fannie Mae DUS Financing for National City Apartments
stories.excerpt: Northmarq’s San Diego Debt + Equity team, led by Aaron Beck and Bryce Quezada, arranged $13.275 million in financing for Talas Apartments, a newly constructed 48-unit multifamily garden community located at 2114 E. 7th St. in National City. Northmarq arranged the refinance on behalf of KIRE Group through the firm’s in-house Fannie Mae DUS platform. The permanent fixed-rate loan is structured on a five-year term with full-term interest-only payments. “This was an all-around great execution for our client as a first-time agency borrower,” Beck said. “The near-stabilization structure allowed the sponsor to exit their construction loan into a fixed-rate permanent loan, return significant equity to their investors, and meaningfully boost monthly cash flow. In a fluctuating rate environment, being able to lock rate and fund prior to reaching stabilized occupancy was a significant win.” Built in 2025, Talas Apartments offers studio, one- and two-bedroom units with modern finishes, in-unit washers and dryers, and private balconies or patios. Join the region’s leading multifamily owners, developers, investors, lenders, and brokers at Connect Texas Multifamily on August 13 in Dallas for a focused discussion on the market forces redefining Texas real estate. Be in the room with the decision-makers driving deals and growth across Texas. Register today: www.ConnectTXMF2026.com. The post Northmarq Secures Fannie Mae DUS Financing for National City Apartments appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Northmarq arranges $13.275M Fannie Mae DUS permanent financing for Talas Apartments (48-unit garden community, National City, California; 2025 completion)
KIRE Group sponsor; 5-year fixed-rate, full-term IO; 'near-stabilization' structure; first-time agency borrower; in-house DUS platform execution
Small multifamily deal; demonstrates Fannie Mae appetite for newly built workforce/garden-style product in secondary markets
stories.summary: Northmarq arranged $13.275 million in Fannie Mae DUS permanent financing for Talas Apartments, a newly constructed 48-unit garden-style community in National City, California. The refinance features a five-year fixed-rate, full-term interest-only structure and represents KIRE Group's entry into agency lending. The near-stabilization structure allowed early exit from construction debt and return of equity to investors.
stories.impact: Fannie Mae's willingness to finance small (48-unit), newly built garden-style product in secondary Southern California markets (National City) signals continued agency liquidity for stabilized, workforce-oriented multifamily. For developers: small-unit garden projects in secondary metros remain fundable via Fannie Mae DUS platforms with strong sponsors; however, loan sizes ($276K per unit) are tight, indicating limited leverage. Expect continued agency appetite for workforce/market-rate product if projected rents support debt service.

stories.title: D.C.’s Trophy Office Leasing Picks Up. The Rest, Not So Much.
stories.excerpt: Washington, D.C.’s office market recovery is becoming more concentrated within the cream of its crop. D.C. office leasing reached roughly 2 million square feet during the second quarter, the highest quarterly volume since 2024, according to Savills. Law firms alone leased about 600,000 square feet, and some large occupiers completed transactions, but the broader market remains hamstrung by empty office space and declining federal leasing. Office tenants are making long-term commitments in D.C. but they’re almost always choosing trophy assets, focusing on new or recently renovated buildings. Availability for D.C.’s Class A buildings was at 10 percent after the second quarter while the city’s overall availability rate increased to 24.2 percent. That increase comes despite 4 millions square feet of space coming off the market the past year from conversions. Similarly, overall asking rents increased 2.9 percent compared to the second quarter of 2025, while Class A rents rose more than 5 percent over the same period to $62.29 per square foot per year. Law firm White & Case signed the quarter’s largest lease in a move to nearly 196,000 square feet at 1701 Pennsylvania Avenue NW, while firms Steptoe, O’Melveny and Wilkinson Barker Knauer committed to long-term renewals. Tech companies also added to the uptick. Palantir renewed and expanded to 150,000 square feet in Georgetown, while Thomson Reuters signed for almost 33,000 square feet in the East End. Savills expects leasing momentum to carry into the second half of the year with deals nearing completion, including leases by Google and the U.S. Navy. Savills also said it expects office-to-residential conversion projects to continue to reduce inventory, particularly while the construction pipeline is low. Gregory Cornfield can be reached at gcornfield@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: D.C. office leasing 2M SF Q2 2026 (highest since 2024); law firms alone 600K SF; Class A vacancy 10%, overall 24.2%; Class A rents +5% YoY to $62.29/SF
4M SF converted to other uses YoY; overall asking rents +2.9% despite elevated vacancies; trophy asset selectivity widening gap vs. secondary stock
Federal leasing declining; flight to quality ongoing; secondary office-to-multifamily conversion opportunity window remains open
stories.summary: Washington, D.C.'s office market is bifurcating: Q2 2026 leasing reached 2 million square feet (highest since 2024), driven by law firms (600,000 SF), but Class A vacancy is only 10 percent while overall market vacancy sits at 24.2 percent. Class A rents rose 5+ percent year-over-year to $62.29/SF, while overall rents rose 2.9 percent. Federal leasing is in structural decline, and 4 million square feet has been converted off the market in the past year.
stories.impact: D.C.'s bifurcation is extreme: trophy office remains leasing at strong growth, while secondary stock languishes. For multifamily developers: secondary/Class B office buildings in non-iconic locations (K Street midblock, NoMa fringe, Rosslyn backfill) are increasingly available for adaptive reuse or cheap land-cost conversions. Lenders may be slow to finance conversion (construction risk + office asset impairment), but equity sponsors and community land trusts have opportunity to acquire distressed or below-market secondary office and convert to workforce multifamily. Window is open now; federal sector decline will persist, and conversion velocities should accelerate if financing unblocks.

stories.title: Azorim Secures $69M Construction Loan for Yonkers Development
stories.excerpt: The fourth and final tower in a decade-long, multiphase development in Westchester County just secured construction capital. Israel-based development firm Azorim has secured $68.75 million in construction financing to build Miroza Tower 4, a 14-story, 174-unit luxury apartment tower that will complete the Miroza at Ridge Hill master-planned development in Yonkers, N.Y. Western Alliance Bank provided the debt, structured as a fixed-rate, interest-only construction loan, while Walker & Dunlop’s Jonathan Zilber, Joel Chetner and Mackenzie Kerin arranged the transaction. Zilber, senior managing director of capital markets real estate finance at Walker & Dunlop, described Azorim as “a best-in-class sponsor” in a statement, and noted that his firm previously arranged $145 million in new financing for the second and third towers of the Miroza development project for Azorim in late 2025. “Despite ongoing market headwinds, our deep familiarity with the sponsorship and ability to optimize the project’s PILOT and market-rate bond structure enabled us to deliver a highly competitive financing solution,” said Zilber. Miroza at Ridge Hill is at 599 Ridge Hill Boulevard in Yonkers, New York’s third-largest city after the Big Apple and Buffalo, and only 15 miles north of Manhattan on the Hudson River. Miroza Tower 4 will deliver 174 units and 248 parking spaces to Ridge Hill, a live-work-play town square near the Grassy Sprain Reservoir and Sprain Ridge Park in Yonkers. The four-building Miroza residential complex totals 520 units and saw the first three towers open in 2013, 2023 and 2024. Miroza’s buildings are connected by a 25,000-square-foot amenity center with a swimming pool, a basketball court, a movie theater and a running track. That connection turns the apartments into a self-contained city, and steps from the well-regarded restaurants and medical center of the Ridge Hill neighborhood. Azorim did not respond to requests for comment. Brian Pascus can be reached at
bullets: Azorim secures $68.75M construction financing (Tower 4, Miroza at Ridge Hill, Yonkers; 14-story, 174-unit luxury apartment tower) from Western Alliance Bank
Final phase of decade-long, four-tower master plan; fixed-rate, IO construction loan; Azorim previously secured $145M for Towers 2–3 in late 2025
Westchester County multifamily capital flowing despite 'market headwinds'; sponsor track record + familiarity key to lender commitment
stories.summary: Azorim, an Israel-based developer, secured $68.75 million in fixed-rate, interest-only construction financing from Western Alliance Bank for Miroza Tower 4, a 174-unit luxury apartment tower that will complete the Miroza at Ridge Hill master-planned community in Yonkers, New York. This is the fourth and final phase of a decade-long development; Azorim previously arranged $145 million in financing for Towers 2–3 in late 2025.
stories.impact: Western Alliance's continued appetite for Azorim (three consecutive financing rounds over 12+ months) demonstrates lender willingness to fund experienced sponsors building large, phased master-planned communities in Westchester County, even amid broader market headwinds. For the Yonkers/Ridge Hill submarket: 174 units of new luxury supply in final phase will add to earlier completions; timing of completion and absorption rates will signal Westchester market health. Developers in Westchester should expect lenders to favor experienced, multi-phase sponsors with proven execution; smaller, single-asset developers may face higher hurdle rates or equity calls.

stories.title: Prime Finance Refis Denver-Area Apartments With $46M Loan
stories.excerpt: A joint venture between Oak Coast Properties and BMC Investments has sealed a $46.25 million loan to refinance a multifamily asset in suburban Denver, Commercial Observer has learned Prime Finance supplied the floating-rate loan on the 388-unit Aspen Park property in Northglenn, Colo. Walker & Dunlop arranged the transaction with a capital markets team consisting of Dustin Stolly, Justin Nelson, Aaron Appel, Jonathan Schwartz, Keith Kurland, Adam Schwartz, Sean Reimer, Michael Ianno, Eric Norris and Jason Schwartzberg. “Aspen Park is well positioned in one of metro Denver’s most connected suburban communities, with strong access to employment, transportation, and everyday amenities,” Stolly, senior managing director of capital markets institutional advisory at Walker & Dunlop, said in a statement. Located at 301 East Malley Drive 12 miles north of Downtown Denver, Aspen Park features one-, two- and three-bedroom garden-style apartments. Community amenities include a clubhouse, a fitness center, an indoor pool, a business center and a game room. Oak Coast Properties, BMC Investments and Prime Finance did not immediately return requests for comment. Andrew Coen can be reached at acoen@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Prime Finance provides $46.25M floating-rate refinance for Aspen Park (388-unit garden-style, Northglenn, Colorado; suburban Denver, 12 miles north of downtown)
Oak Coast Properties and BMC Investments co-own; Walker & Dunlop arranged; strong amenities (pool, clubhouse, fitness center); connected suburban location cited
Floating-rate execution indicates sponsor belief in rate decline or stability; Denver metro apartment demand remains solid despite new supply
stories.summary: Oak Coast Properties and BMC Investments refinanced Aspen Park, a 388-unit garden-style apartment community in Northglenn, Colorado, with a $46.25 million floating-rate loan from Prime Finance arranged by Walker & Dunlop. The property is 12 miles north of Downtown Denver, with strong amenities and one-, two-, and three-bedroom units. Lender highlighted the property's positioning in a connected suburban community.
stories.impact: Prime Finance's floating-rate execution on a garden-style suburban product suggests sponsor comfort with rate outlook and belief in Denver metro rental demand sustainability. For Denver metro: Northglenn's appeal to institutional capital and lenders (job access, transportation, amenities) indicates strong underlying demand in suburban ring; garden-style suburban multifamily continues to attract capital. Developers should note: floating-rate financing at current levels (<150 bps over SOFR) is available for stabilized, well-located suburban product with strong ownership; however, rate path will dictate pricing and term length going forward.
